classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

title: Pain Only in Good Natures.

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But pain which some suppose to be in an especial manner an evil, whether it be in mind or in body, cannot exist except in good natures.

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For the very fact of resistance in any being leading to pain, involves a refusal not to be what it was, because it was something good; but when a being is compelled to something better, the pain is useful, when to something worse, it is useless.

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Therefore in the case of the mind, the will resisting a greater power causes pain; in the case of the body, sensation resisting a more powerful body causes pain.

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But evils without pain are worse: for it is worse to rejoice iniquity than to bewail corruption; yet even such rejoicing cannot exist save from the attainment of inferior good things.

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But iniquity is the desertion of better things.

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Likewise in a body, a wound with pain is better than painless putrescence, which is especially called the corruption which the dead flesh of the Lord did not see, that is, did not suffer, as was predicted in prophecy: "Thou shall not suffer Thy Holy one to see corruption."

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For who denies that He was wounded by the piercing of the nails, and that He was stabbed with the lance?

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But even what is properly called by men corporeal corruption, that is, putrescence itself, if as yet there is anything left to consume, increases by the diminution of the good.

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But if corruption shall have absolutely consumed it, so that there is no good, no nature will remain, for there will be nothing that corruption may corrupt; and so there will not even be putrescence, for there will be nowhere at all for it to be.
